speaker: interviewer
text: OK, quiet, everyone, please. Getting up to speed. And doing the slate. And go ahead, Mary.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. My name is Mary Kasden. Today's date is August 8, 1995. I'm conducting an interview with survivor Esther Barishman. The interview is being conducted in Laguna Hills, California, USA. Getting up to speed. And [INAUDIBLE]. All right. Could you begin by telling us your name, with the spelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther Barishman. B-A-R-I-S-H-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And your name at birth. Your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden name is Zucker.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth was Esther, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther, but I had a nickname by Tunia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Your birth date.

speaker: interviewee
text: October 19, 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your age.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and the city-- with the spelling-- that you were born in.

speaker: interviewee
text: The city is Zdolbunow. Z-D-O-L-B-U-N-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the country.

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, good, thank you. All right, I'd like to start with your life before the war. And could you tell me a little bit about your parents, first? What type of work your father did, and what your mother did.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was-- he had different kind of business. Like, once he had a factory from soda that you-- soda water. But then later he was in transportation business. He transferred from Zdolbunow to Rowne-- he was driving. My mother was a housewife. But she also was making cream, for face cream. And she was selling that and was very successful. As well, she was a good housewife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And sewing-- she was sewing for us children by herself. In those days, you didn't-- you didn't buy clothes from the store. You just sew it from materials and made clothes. So she did all the sewing for us children. What else? Your father-- with the transportation company, was he always the one who was driving? Or did he eventually have employees?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had employees, and were driving, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was it like a bus company?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Buses. OK. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what would you say your economic status was, before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before, it was middle class.

speaker: interviewer
text: Middle class?

speaker: interviewee
text: Middle class, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned sometimes you would-- oh, go ahead. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were four children, at home, and all four of us went to private Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is an expense, you know? We were dressed well, we had what to eat, but we weren't rich. We were just making it go.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think you mentioned to me that you had some relatives in America who would sometimes send things.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My mother had a brother in America. And he used to send us parcels, like for Rosh Hashanah, for Passover, presents. And so we had a little help from him. I mean, all of his brothers and sisters-- He was very well-to-do in the United States, in New York. So he used to send parcels for his families that I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he sometimes send material?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, material, so she was sewing for us children, everything, herself, my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she sewed all your clothes, pretty much?

speaker: interviewee
text: She sewed all our clothes by herself.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Very nice. OK. Your father's interests? Was he interested in acting?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was an amateur actor. He was a very big Zionist. He worked for the Hebrew community, with [NON-ENGLISH]. He was very active in the Jewish organizations. As well, in 1925 my father-- being a Zionist-- with the family, like my mother and my-- my older sister and my brother, he-- he went to Palestine to help build-- build this country. And he was there for two years. But the circumstances were too poor. He didn't have a good job. The heat was too-- too big for my mother and the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they returned back to Poland. The return, I think, was in 1927-- beginning of 1927. So they returned back. I wish they wouldn't do that. All right. Your father was also active in sports, to some extent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Like football. Just amateur, not professionally.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell us about the school that you went to.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a private Hebrew school. First in kindergarten, which was only for Jewish children too-- it's a Jewish kindergarten-- then to private Hebrew school. Went all the way till the end of-- till 1941. In 1939, the Russian occupied our place. Our-- so we went to-- it wasn't a Hebrew school, it was a Yiddish school. Because Hebrew wasn't allowed by Russian to-- to mention, even, Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was Yiddish. We learned Yiddish and Russian. And by Poland, we learned-- everything was in Hebrew. It just-- one hour a day, it was Polish-- just the language-- to learn. But geography and everything else was in Hebrew language. So in 1939 the Russian occupied our place and turned over to Yiddish. And I went to school till '41, until the Germans came in to occupy it. Then was no more schooling-- nothing anymore. So for the two years between '39, when Germany invaded Poland, and '41, your area was not that affected that much by the Germans? More by the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We had a free life, from 1939 to 1941, we were-- just occupation was the Russian. But we had a-- a normal life. There was nothing wrong-- and some--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us, again, exactly where the town is that you were in.

speaker: interviewee
text: The town is between Kiev and Lviv-- and between. It was 33 kilometer from the Russian border. Which the border was Ostrog. And this was 33 kilometers, Zdolbunow.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK. Still, before 1941, when you were at school, everyone in the school was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Hebrew school. It was a Hebrew school. All of us were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: For boys and girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Boys and girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so your siblings went there, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. All four children, we went. As a matter of fact, not only all four-- the population of the Jewish people went to Hebrew school. Just a small fraction of very poor that couldn't afford to pay for the Hebrew school went to Polish school, or the assimilated Jews that didn't want to acknowledge that they are Jews. They are the ones that went to Polish school. But most-- 95% of the Jews went to Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Now, um-- let's  see, I lost my thought, there. Then you were religious-- your family-- your father was interested--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: We weren't religious. My father was free. A Zionist, that's not religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. So then you did not go to a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, we went only the High Holidays in the synagogue. But we were not religious at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you maybe the equivalent of Reform Judaism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So did you celebrate many of the holidays, or just the High Holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: The High Holidays, and the Jewish-- like, all the Jewish holidays. But we didn't go to shul every day, or every Friday. My father didn't go to shul. It's just, um--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm. So that your going to Hebrew school wasn't so much because your parents were that observant, it was because that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What everyone did, because you had the cultural identity of being Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And at home-- tell me about the kind of books and things that you had at home-- what you can remember. Maybe not so much things having to do with Judaism, but just literature, in general, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Literature in general. Literature in general.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you knew Hebrew quite well, then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- then, when I came to Israel, sure. I knew Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: From your school, even when you were a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: From school-- right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents didn't know it as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father knew-- my mother didn't, but my father knew Hebrew, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: From when he was in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: From school that he learned.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he knew Hebrew, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the children were really fluent in both Hebrew and Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Yiddish, mostly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish and Polish and Hebrew. But just at school we spoke the Hebrew language. At home we spoke Yiddish-- and Polish, eventually. But mainly Yiddish at home we spoke.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Is there anything you can tell me about how learning your basic subjects in Hebrew would be different than learning them in Polish or in English?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's been the same. It's just the language is in Hebrew. We had all-- we had geometry, we had geography, we had language, and everything. But, instead of being in Polish, it was in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now did you belong to any Jewish youth organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Hashomer Hatzair as a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe any of the activities-- the types of things you did in that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we used to get together a specific place and associate with each other. Mind you, I was still a little girl when the time-- was until 1941. I was still young. But I remember the Hebrew organization-- I mean the Hashomer Hatzair. We associated. And they-- Friday night and Saturday mornings. Because in Poland we didn't have, like, the weekends-- Saturday and Sunday off. We had school. We didn't go to school on Saturday, but we went on Sunday. The Polish people didn't go on Sunday to school, but they did go on Saturday to school. So this was the difference between the Jews and the Pole, because in the wintertime where we went on Sunday to school, and they went to church. So we got some snowballs they were throwing at us. Because they knew that this is Jewish children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was one of the times that there would be a difference between you, and you might have experienced the anti-Semitism outside of school.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other experiences did you have with the anti-Semitism before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was a lot, even. It was written on the poles 'Zydzi do Palestyny.' That means 'Jews, go to Palestine.' And 'we hate Jews.' It was written all over. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: When we went to school, we were afraid to see the Polish boys-- Gentile kids-- that we were beaten up on the way, going to school. We didn't drove to school, we had to walk to school, you know? So on every sign, the anti-Semitism was there. And because we were Jewish kids, because we went to different schools, they knew it right away that we are Jews. Now what about your father and his business? How did he deal with anti-Semitism? Did he experience it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he had no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think he had less?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know why. But-- well, to tell the truth, everywhere and anywhere it was the anti-Semitism there. You know? Some showed it more, some showed it less. But the anti-Semitism was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Just tell us about your brothers-- your brother? Excuse me, I'm forgetting. There were four children altogether. Would you just list your siblings, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had an older sister. She was Shulamid Zucker. She-- she was a gorgeous woman-- young lady. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And she was born in 1921. Then next came my brother, which is now in New York. He was born in 1923. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then I came in 1927. And a younger sister than me, Rachel-- she was in 1929 born.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Is there anything that you can remember that you'd like to say about your sisters or your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my--

speaker: interviewer
text: From childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest sister was a very good-looking young lady. She had beautiful white teeth. She went and-- she-- I remember her going already to university, to college, in Rowne. Rowne was 12 miles-- 12 kilometers away from Zdolbunow, where there was a old-- where there was a Hebrew school, a Hebrew college, just-- because not everywhere Jews were accepted in college. So in Rowne was a Hebrew college. My brother was-- is-- was the only one son in the family, which my mother adored. He's-- the little boy. He-- he was-- he left-- he left to Russia before Hitler arrived. Like a day before Hitler, I mean, occupied our city, he left the day before to Russia. And he survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me why he left and no one else left. How did it happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was-- we now-- specific place-- my father-- being under the Russian regime, my father was-- had a grocery store. Not he had-- he was running a grocery store for a manager of a grocery store. And apparently he sold-- he sold a little bit-- he took some-- you know, like, less than he was supposed to take home. And he was arrested-- by the Russian. He was arrested and sent away to a different town, to work there instead of being with the family. This was his punishment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father. And when the-- when-- when the Germans occupied our place, my father wasn't home. So if he would be home, possible that the whole family will go to Russia. But because he wasn't home, my brother left by himself, and we remained. And a few days later, my father was walking about 50 miles, walking home. And on the way to walk home, he-- a German drunk fella-- man, soldier, recognized that he's a Jew. And he was very drunk, and he gave him a shovel to make his own grave. And my father was making his own grave, and he stood by with him, with  that gun in his hand. He said, Jew, you make your own grave. And soon he digged all the grave out. He give him a-- he slapped him with the shovel, and he said, run, Jew run! And my father run away. And that's the way he survived. And he came back to home, to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were already under occupation of Germany, and we couldn't go anywhere. I was taken right away in the ghetto, and-- But when your brother left, was that because they thought that your brother was particularly vulnerable, being a boy? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, everybody-- everybody-- anybody that want to go to Russia was-- the Russian open up the doors-- come, come to us. Run away. But nobody thought that it's that bad, that it will be the that bad that we have to leave the house. You know? Many people did leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made him go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because hearing what's going on, he was afraid of the German occupation, and he ran away. Also, he was working in a-- in a-- from the job, they ask him-- they ask everybody, you want to come with us to Russia? Let's go. So he just picked up and went.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we couldn't go, because the father wasn't home.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you said your father was put-- you said, put in jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not a jail. It was-- he was sent away in a different city to work, not to be in the same city with his family. It was like a--

speaker: interviewer
text: And who sent him?

speaker: interviewee
text: The court-- I mean, from court. There was a court, and this was--

speaker: interviewer
text: The civil court.

speaker: interviewee
text: His punishment. The civil court. This was the punishment.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean he took home a little too much. I thought you said a little too little.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something he did make-- handle one-- something he did-- something not legally. I don't know exactly if he sold too much or he took home something. Something not legal. It's little things. It's not-- for big things, they send to Siberia. But this was something small.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when did you see your brother again? After the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My brother-- we met in 1945, in August, I think it was-- August. May was-- we were liberated. And August we met in Lodz together.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When were you first aware of the war? What do you remember hearing first?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we heard, like, the airplanes were going and throwing bombs and-- just got to know right away what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: From '39 on?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '39 on, yeah. In '39. '39, it wasn't hard for us, because the Russian occupied our place. So we lived a normal life, and everything was available and there was no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bu you could still hear some bombing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Later, you heard it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Quiet-- it was quiet, and we lived a normal life like nothing happened. As a matter of fact, in some places the Russian did many better than it was by Poland, you know? Like, it was more work available, more food available.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we lived a normal life. From 1939 till 1941 was nothing wrong. That's when the German took over, then, when it-- Between '39 and '41, do you remember listening to the radio or anything like that-- about the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We knew it's going on-- a war. But we didn't feel it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it seem distant, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Distant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Something that wouldn't touch you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now tell me how your life started to change, in '41. What were the first signs of change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you mean when the German took--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Occupied our places? Well, first of all they stopped school right away. It was no more school. From beginning, we were still in our home-- in our homes. Not for a while. So it was, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: They put some yellow [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH], what do you call it-- from the back and the front. We had some yellow-- stamps-- what do you call it? The [NON-ENGLISH]. Patch.

speaker: interviewee
text: Patch. The yellow patches. We had to wear-- young and old and everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a star?

speaker: interviewee
text: The star.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not a patch. It was a star we had on my arm. But yellow patches on back and front.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say back and front, you mean on your back?

speaker: interviewee
text: On your back and on the front. So when you go from both sides, it'll be seen that you are Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: A round patch, or something?

speaker: interviewee
text: A round, yellow patch. You didn't hear about it? Did you wear it? Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had that, and you had the star.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or that or the star.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. One or the other.

speaker: interviewee
text: One or the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about in freedom? Did they decrease your freedom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. We had no freedom at all. I mean, we were Jews! We had nothing to say, nothing to do. They took away right away the business from people. Even business-- As far as business is concerned, the Russian people took away the business from the Jewish people, too. The big business. It was under communism. But when the German-- the Germany occupation, it's-- you felt right away that you are a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as a child, you were wearing the patch or the armband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you were allowed to be on the sidewalks or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there certain playgrounds you couldn't go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We weren't allowed on the sidewalks. We weren't allowed in the playgrounds. We were-- Even as a child, you didn't have your freedom.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When was the ghetto established in your town? How soon after the Germans came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto's about a half a year after they came in. I would say, like, in the spring of '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- what am I saying, '43-- it's not '43. '42 was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: '41, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spring of '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was your family sent to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We all were sent to the ghetto. We were living in one little room, six people of us. We were in one room. We had to go to work. Every day, one of us had to be at work, like-- in different-- my father was working, and my mother was supposed to work. And one of my sisters-- because I wasn't supposed to work. I was too young. But my sister, my mother, and father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father worked on the railroads. My mother-- I went to work because-- to not for-- instead of my mother, I was going to work. So wherever they sent you, it wasn't a particular job that you're doing-- a regular job. Wherever they sent you-- today on the railroad, tomorrow to digging graves, to-- another day-- whatever they had available-- to clean the-- the-- this housing, you know? Every time was a different job to do. How did you do it? Did you gather at a certain place in the morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We gathered, and they-- under the-- under a-- the German soldier was bringing us to work whatever we had to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you were really too-- you were about 14, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that time, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In '41. And you were really too young to work, but they let you go in place of your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway. They let me go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was supervising that work? Was that SS men, or Ukrainians, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ukrainian, SS men, it was no different. Not everybody-- anybody and everybody, as long as you had a uniform on-- Ukrainian or SS men or the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you said you were digging graves, was that just regular grave work? Or were they already killing people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they killed people in our place, in our city of Zdolbunow. They just make one killing, and that's it--

speaker: interviewer
text: At the end of-- near the end of the ghetto

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the end of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So that was just regular grave work, in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah--

speaker: interviewer
text: In cemeteries-- places like that. OK, could you describe your living conditions in the ghetto? How big your room was. You mentioned six people?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were six people. No, actually, not six-- we were five, because my brother was away. We were five people in one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how big was the room?

speaker: interviewee
text: How big was the room? About half the size as this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Half the size of this living room. Pretty small room.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Pretty small room. There was just two beds and a couch. And there-- and that's all. And a table.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Was the ghetto itself physically closed off from the rest of the city? Were their fences, or walls, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Fences.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fences.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was fenced up. Yes, it was fenced up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there soldiers at the fences?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were soldiers staying, and there-- and to let in or out, you had to be identified. Ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you gather to go to work outside the gate? Or where would you be picked up to go out to work? Or was the work within the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside the gate, and they bring you over the gate.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what kind of vehicle would you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Walking. There was no vehicles. It was walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sometimes long distances?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes long distances, too. But we didn't have any transportation. It was walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Your food? What kind of food in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food was very limited-- very limited. Very limited. Just bread and-- I mean, you get rationed bread. You snuck, like, a-- you took it in your house-- bread, and I don't know what else. Sugar, whatever, I don't remember all those things. But very, very-- very little food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there stores in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- I don't remember there were stores in the ghetto. No. I really don't remember how it came. How they rationed out the-- the-- the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'm going to stop you here so we can change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
